narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and a middle-income family background, you've put together a thoughtful application to law school, including your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 91% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in typical successful applicants. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected; the school uses this model to prioritize students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which places you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year—much lower than the average of around the middle of the pack—and strongly signals potential challenges ahead on high-stakes exams like the bar. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again well below the typical mid-range performance, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength early on. Your undergraduate GPA offered some counterbalance, as it's close to the average of 3.21 and nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing you have capability in college-level work. While your LSAT score of 30 is below the dataset average of 36.7, it wasn't the top driver here; instead, those early low decile rankings dominated the model's view of your bar exam readiness compared to peers who typically rank higher throughout school. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many students overcome similar starts—but the model weighs these patterns heavily based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above average, strengthening your high school or early college record through additional coursework or certifications, and seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the prediction in your favor, as even small improvements in rankings and test scores make a big difference for applicants like you. You're clearly motivated, and with focused steps, law school remains within reach.
